pattern: The count of full tilings of a two-row strip obeys a short linear recurrence because each new column is completed either by closing it flush or by leaving a single overhanging cell that an L-shaped piece will later fill.
hints: Build the board column by column and track whether the boundary between the filled part and the rest is flat or has one extra cell sticking out.
Let f(n) be the number of ways to tile a flush 2-by-n region; relating f(n) to smaller flush and jagged boundaries gives a fixed linear recurrence.
The recurrence f(n) = 2*f(n-1) + f(n-3) holds with f(0)=1, f(1)=1, f(2)=2; iterate it modulo 1e9+7 up to n.

edge_inputs.description: width one has a single vertical domino
value large enough to exercise the modulo wrap

stress.description: random widths over the full allowed range
stress.note: the linear recurrence handles any n within range instantly

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def numTilings(self, n):
        MOD = 10 ** 9 + 7
        # full[i] = ways to fully tile 2xi; partial[i] = ways with one cell of column i protruding
        if n == 0:
            return 1
        full = [0] * (n + 1)
        partial = [0] * (n + 1)
        full[0] = 1
        full[1] = 1
        partial[1] = 0
        if n >= 1:
            pass
        for i in range(2, n + 1):
            full[i] = (full[i - 1] + full[i - 2] + 2 * partial[i - 1]) % MOD
            partial[i] = (full[i - 2] + partial[i - 1]) % MOD
        return full[n] % MOD
